message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (fully closes it; for a code change the named regression test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if the fix were reverted). Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix G-012 in src/assay_engine/provenance.py
What: from_records raises typed ProvenanceError (not raw KeyError) on a record missing a required field.
Test(s): test_from_records_raises_typed_error_on_malformed_record
Steps: read the actual changed file(s); read the test and judge whether it discriminates; check no regression. For a doc/refactor item, judge correctness/coverage rather than revert-discrimination. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN (something missing/wrong/under-tested). Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify the G-012 fix. Let me start by examining the changed file and test.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git log --oneline -8 && echo "---" && git log --all --oneline | grep -i "G-012" | head
input.description: Show recent commits and find G-012

content.content:
7dccfd8 fix(hardening-p4): batch E — egress, error contracts, PII & test discrimination (8 findings)
c49261e fix(hardening-p4): batch D — CI / supply-chain reproducibility (G-015,016,022)
2eea958 fix(hardening-p4): batch C — provenance, observability & resample memory (G-009,010,012,023)
91dfb1a fix(hardening-p4): batch B — concurrency, reasoning-tier & serialization (G-002,003,004)
d61bd4e fix(hardening-p4): batch A — methodology integrity (G-001,005,006,011,017)
0788961 docs(hardening): pass-3 merged (#161); open pass-4 with retrospective dimensions
54cd88c Merge pull request #161 from thehipsterciso/harden/pass-3
5f16960 docs(hardening): record pass-3 (49/54 confirmed, all fixed/reclassified) + pass-4 dimensions
---

commit 2eea9584d8151b0893d19cb580c69508492870a9
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Tue Jun 16 23:56:24 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p4): batch C — provenance, observability & resample memory (G-009,010,012,023)
    
    Pass-4 confirmed findings (2-agent verified, revert-discriminated where applicable):
    
    - G-009 (medium) run_study now persists the hash-chained provenance trail as a JSON
      artifact and correlates it to the MLflow run (tracker.log_artifact), so an auditor
      opening a run can retrieve the provenance behind its metrics. Best-effort, like the
      other tracker calls.
    - G-010 (medium) the numpy resample-stability path uses searchsorted on a sorted null
      (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) instead of an R×N boolean matrix that peaked at
      ~250MB for R=5000,N=50000 and could OOM. Integer counts are identical (the 300-case
      numpy==pure equivalence test still passes).
    - G-012 (medium) from_records raises the typed ProvenanceError (not a raw KeyError) on a
      record missing a required field, so all trail-deserialization failures surface as one
      catchable error.
    - G-023 (low) the corpus unit-id set is computed once and reused for the split-id and
      feature-id validations (was built twice on the same corpus).
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; 509 passed, 5 skipped.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/provenance.py b/src/assay_engine/provenance.py
index e3f0d76..7a76678 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/provenance.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/provenance.py
@@ -263,17 +263,26 @@ def from_records(
     secret: bytes | None = None,
 ) -> tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]:
     """Rebuild entries from :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.to_records` output and verify the chain."""
-    entries = tuple(
-        ProvenanceEntry(
-            seq=r["seq"],
-            kind=r["kind"],
-            summary=r["summary"],
-            payload=r["payload"],
-            timestamp=r["timestamp"],
-            prev_hash=r["prev_hash"],
-            entry_hash=r["entry_hash"],
+    _required = ("seq", "kind", "summary", "payload", "timestamp", "prev_hash", "entry_hash")
+    rebuilt: list[ProvenanceEntry] = []
+    for i, r in enumerate(records):
+        missing = [k for k in _required if k not in r]
+        if missing:
+            # A malformed/incompatible trail record must surface as the module's typed error, not a
+            # raw KeyError, so a caller catching ProvenanceError to handle a corrupt trail file
+            # covers this case too (pass 4, #G-012).
+            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance record {i} is missing field(s) {missing}")
+        rebuilt.append(
+            ProvenanceEntry(
+                seq=r["seq"],
+                kind=r["kind"],
+                summary=r["summary"],
+                payload=r["payload"],
+                timestamp=r["timestamp"],
+                prev_hash=r["prev_hash"],
+                entry_hash=r["entry_hash"],
+            )
         )
-        for r in records
-    )
+    entries = tuple(rebuilt)
     verify_records(entries, secret=secret)
     return entries

content.text: Now let me read the current file state and the test.

content.content:
255	                f"provenance entry {i} content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)"
256	            )
257	        prev = e.entry_hash
258	
259	
260	def from_records(
261	    records: "list[Mapping[str, Any]] | tuple[Mapping[str, Any], ...]",
262	    *,
263	    secret: bytes | None = None,
264	) -> tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]:
265	    """Rebuild entries from :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.to_records` output and verify the chain."""
266	    _required = ("seq", "kind", "summary", "payload", "timestamp", "prev_hash", "entry_hash")
267	    rebuilt: list[ProvenanceEntry] = []
268	    for i, r in enumerate(records):
269	        missing = [k for k in _required if k not in r]
270	        if missing:
271	            # A malformed/incompatible trail record must surface as the module's typed error, not a
272	            # raw KeyError, so a caller catching ProvenanceError to handle a corrupt trail file
273	            # covers this case too (pass 4, #G-012).
274	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance record {i} is missing field(s) {missing}")
275	        rebuilt.append(
276	            ProvenanceEntry(
277	                seq=r["seq"],
278	                kind=r["kind"],
279	                summary=r["summary"],
280	                payload=r["payload"],
281	                timestamp=r["timestamp"],
282	                prev_hash=r["prev_hash"],
283	                entry_hash=r["entry_hash"],
284	            )
285	        )
286	    entries = tuple(rebuilt)
287	    verify_records(entries, secret=secret)
288	    return entries
289

1	"""Append-only provenance trail (GOVERNANCE §3, ADR-0010) + the hardening regressions."""
2	
3	from __future__ import annotations
4	
5	import datetime as _dt
6	
7	import pytest
8	
9	from assay_engine.provenance import (
10	    ProvenanceError,
11	    ProvenanceTrail,
12	    from_records,
13	)
14	
15	_UTC = _dt.timezone.utc
16	
17	
18	def _fixed_clock():
19	    t = _dt.datetime(2026, 6, 16, 12, 0, 0, tzinfo=_UTC)
20	    return lambda: t
21	
22	
23	# ---- append-only + chain integrity ----
24	
25	
26	def test_records_chain_and_verify():
27	    t = ProvenanceTrail()
28	    t.record("a", "first", x=1)
29	    t.record("b", "second", y=[1, 2])
30	    t.verify()
31	    assert len(t) == 2 and t.entries[0].prev_hash != t.entries[1].prev_hash
32	
33	
34	def test_from_records_raises_typed_error_on_malformed_record():
35	    # #G-012: a record missing a required field must raise the typed ProvenanceError (so a caller
36	    # catching it to handle a corrupt/incompatible trail covers this), not a raw KeyError.
37	    good = ProvenanceTrail()
38	    good.record("a", "first", x=1)
39	    records = list(good.to_records())
40	    del records[0]["summary"]  # corrupt the record
41	    with pytest.raises(ProvenanceError, match="missing field"):
42	        from_records(records)
43	
44	
45	def test_entries_view_is_immutable_tuple():
46	    t = ProvenanceTrail()
47	    t.record("a", "x")
48	    assert isinstance(t.entries, tuple)
49	    with pytest.raises((TypeError, AttributeError)):
50	        t.entries[0] = None  # type: ignore[index]
51	
52	
53	def test_naive_tamper_is_detected_unkeyed():
54	    t = ProvenanceTrail(clock=_fixed_clock())
55	    t.record("a", "one")
56	    t.record("verdict", "H1: SUPPORTED", label="SUPPORTED")
57	    recs = list(t.to_records())
58	    recs[1] = {**recs[1], "payload": {"label": "CONTRADICTED"}}  # edit, do NOT recompute hash
59	    with pytest.raises(ProvenanceError):
60	        from_records(recs)
61	    # reorder/delete
62	    with pytest.raises(ProvenanceError):
63	        from_records(recs[:1] + recs[2:] if len(recs) > 2 else recs[1:])
64	
65	
66	# ---- #88: keyed (HMAC) trail is forgery-resistant; unkeyed is honestly only naive-evident ----
67	
68	
69	def test_keyed_trail_cannot_be_reforged_without_secret():
70	    secret = b"provenance-seal-secret-0123456789"

content.text: The test looks discriminating. It deletes a required field (`summary`) and expects `ProvenanceError` with "missing field". Let me verify the test passes now, and that it fails (raises raw KeyError) if reverted.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && ls .venv/bin/python* 2>/dev/null; which pytest 2>/dev/null; ls venv 2>/dev/null; head -5 pyproject.toml 2>/dev/null

content.text: Now the discrimination check — revert the fix and confirm the test fails (with a raw KeyError, not ProvenanceError).

cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && cp src/assay_engine/provenance.py /tmp/prov_bak.py && cat > /tmp/patch.py <<'EOF'
import re
p="src/assay_engine/provenance.py"
s=open(p).read()
old=s[s.index('    _required ='):s.index('    entries = tuple(rebuilt)\n')+len('    entries = tuple(rebuilt)\n')]
new='''    entries = tuple(
        ProvenanceEntry(
            seq=r["seq"],
            kind=r["kind"],
            summary=r["summary"],
            payload=r["payload"],
            timestamp=r["timestamp"],
            prev_hash=r["prev_hash"],
            entry_hash=r["entry_hash"],
        )
        for r in records
    )
'''
s=s.replace(old,new)
open(p,"w").write(s)
print("patched")
EOF
.venv/bin/python /tmp/patch.py

cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_provenance.py::test_from_records_raises_typed_error_on_malformed_record -q 2>&1 | tail -20; echo "=== restoring ==="; cp /tmp/prov_bak.py src/assay_engine/provenance.py && git diff --stat src/assay_engine/provenance.py

content.text: Discrimination confirmed: reverted code raises raw `KeyError`, test fails. Restored cleanly. Let me confirm restoration and run the full provenance test file for regressions.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git status --short && echo "---" && .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_provenance.py -q 2>&1 | tail -8
input.description: Verify clean tree and full provenance tests

content.text:
Tree is clean (restore matched committed state exactly), all 16 provenance tests pass.

Assessment:

Appropriate:
- The fix iterates records, checks each against the `_required` tuple of all seven fields, and raises `ProvenanceError` with a clear message naming the index and missing fields before constructing `ProvenanceEntry`. Correct and idiomatic.
- `ProvenanceError` is the module's typed error already used throughout (e.g., `verify_records`), so callers catching it for corrupt trails now cover this case — exactly the stated intent.
- No behavioral regression: well-formed records still build identical entries (same dict accesses), then `verify_records` runs as before. Full file passes; commit reports ruff/mypy --strict clean and 509 passed.

Complete:
- Test `test_from_records_raises_typed_error_on_malformed_record` deletes a required field (`summary`) and asserts `pytest.raises(ProvenanceError, match="missing field")`.
- Discrimination verified empirically: with the fix reverted, the test fails with raw `KeyError: 'summary'`; with the fix, it passes. The test genuinely guards the fix (and the `match="missing field"` ensures it's the typed-error path, not some incidental ProvenanceError).

One minor note (not a concern): the check only covers missing keys, not wrong-typed values (e.g., a `seq` of wrong type would still surface however `ProvenanceEntry`/`verify_records` handle it). But the finding G-012 is specifically scoped to "missing required field," which is fully and discriminatingly addressed.

input.reasoning: Fix in src/assay_engine/provenance.py from_records (lines 266-285) checks each record against a _required tuple of all seven fields and raises the module's typed ProvenanceError ("provenance record {i} is missing field(s) {missing}") before constructing ProvenanceEntry. APPROPRIATE: correct, idiomatic (reuses the same ProvenanceError used by verify_records elsewhere), and no regression — well-formed records build identical entries and still pass through verify_records; full provenance suite passes 16/16. COMPLETE + DISCRIMINATING: test_from_records_raises_typed_error_on_malformed_record (tests/test_provenance.py:34) deletes the required 'summary' field and asserts pytest.raises(ProvenanceError, match="missing field"). I empirically reverted the fix to the pre-fix generator expression and the test FAILED with raw KeyError: 'summary'; with the fix it PASSES. The match="missing field" string ties the assertion to the new code path. Restored the file; git tree clean. Scope of G-012 (missing required field) is fully closed; type-validation of present-but-wrong values is out of scope for this finding.

content.text: Verdict: CONFIRMED. The G-012 fix is both appropriate and complete, with a discriminating regression test (verified by revert: raw KeyError without the fix, passes with it).
